---
title: "Experiments"
---

# Experiments

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Plain-Language Summary
This section describes the progression of experiments that led to the
current best result. Each experiment was a deliberate test of one specific
hypothesis. Reading these in order reveals the key insights: the architecture
works, but only with the right initialisation strategy — and the depth of
that initialisation matters more than expected.
:::

---

## Experimental Design Principles {#sec-exp-design}

All experiments share the following design constraints to ensure valid
comparison:

- **Same gold dataset** — all experiments use the 9,660-record billable
  gold layer derived from MedSynth FY2026.
- **Same deterministic splits** — the 80/10/10 train/val/test split with
  seed=42 is fixed for all experiments.
- **Same test set** — the 966-record test set is held out and touched
  only once per experiment for final evaluation.
- **Same evaluation metrics** — all results report top-1 accuracy and
  Macro F1 on the test set, with ECE and Coverage@0.7 for calibrated models.
- **Logged provenance** — every run is logged to `outputs/experiments.json`
  and `outputs/run.log` with full parameter records.

---

## Understanding the Evaluation Metrics {#sec-exp-metrics}

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Plain-Language Summary
Before presenting the experiment results, this section explains what the
evaluation metrics actually measure and why we chose them. This is
particularly important for a 1,926-class problem where the choice of
averaging method significantly affects what the number means — and whether
it honestly reflects the system's capabilities.
:::

---

### Top-1 Accuracy

The simplest metric: of all test records, what fraction did the model
predict correctly?

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{\text{Correct predictions}}{\text{Total predictions}} = \frac{811}{966} = 83.9\%$$

**What it tells you:** How often the system gets the right answer.

**What it conceals:** Whether errors are evenly distributed across all
1,926 codes, or concentrated in a small number of difficult ones.

In multi-class classification, accuracy is equivalent to the **micro F1
score** — it pools all predictions together and measures overall volume
of correct answers. A model that perfectly predicts the 100 most common
codes and fails on the remaining 1,826 could still achieve high accuracy
if the common codes dominate the test set.

In our case the test set is **uniform** (approximately 5 records per code),
so accuracy is a fair metric here. On real clinical data — where hypertension,
diabetes, and back pain dominate — it would be dangerously misleading.

---

### F1 Score: The Building Block

**F1 score** for a single class is the harmonic mean of precision and recall:

$$F1 = 2 \times \frac{\text{Precision} \times \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$$

Where precision and recall are computed using a **One-vs-Rest** approach —
each class is treated as a binary classification problem in turn:

- **Precision** — of all the times the model predicted this code, how
  often was it correct? A high-precision model avoids false alarms.
- **Recall** — of all the records that truly had this code, how many
  did the model find? A high-recall model avoids missed detections.

For a concrete example using `I10` (Essential hypertension):

| | Predicted I10 | Predicted other |
|---|---|---|
| **True I10** | 5 TP | 1 FN |
| **True other** | 0 FP | 960 TN |

$$\text{Precision}_{I10} = \frac{5}{5+0} = 1.00 \qquad
  \text{Recall}_{I10} = \frac{5}{5+1} = 0.833 \qquad
  F1_{I10} = 0.909$$

This per-class F1 is computed separately for all 1,926 codes. The question
is then: how do we average 1,926 F1 scores into a single number?

---

### Three Averaging Strategies — and Why They Give Different Answers

There are three standard approaches, and the differences between them
matter enormously for a 1,926-class problem:

**Macro average** computes the simple unweighted mean across all classes —
every class counts equally regardless of how many examples it has:

$$\text{Macro F1} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} F1_i = \frac{F1_{I10} + F1_{M25.562} + \cdots + F1_{Z79.4}}{1926}$$

**Weighted average** weights each class by its number of examples (its
*support*). Classes with more training examples contribute more to the
final score:

$$\text{Weighted F1} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{\text{support}_i}{\text{total}} \times F1_i$$

**Micro average** pools all True Positives, False Positives, and False
Negatives across all classes before computing F1. For single-label
classification this is mathematically identical to accuracy:

$$\text{Micro F1} = \text{Accuracy} = \frac{\sum TP_i}{\sum (TP_i + FP_i)} = 83.9\%$$

The table below summarises when each is appropriate:

| Averaging | Treats classes | Best when |
|---|---|---|
| **Macro** | Equally | All classes matter equally regardless of frequency |
| **Weighted** | Proportionally to frequency | Common classes matter more |
| **Micro** | By prediction volume | Only overall throughput matters |

---

### Why This Project Uses Macro F1

**Macro F1 is the right choice here for two reasons that reinforce each other.**

First, **all 1,926 ICD-10 codes are clinically equally important.** A rare
code for a serious condition — say `A06.1` (chronic intestinal amoebiasis)
— is no less significant than a common code for a routine condition like
`M25.562` (pain in left knee). Getting the rare code wrong has the same
consequence as getting the common code wrong. Macro averaging encodes this
equal importance directly.

Second, **the training set is deliberately uniform.** MedSynth has
approximately 5 records per code by design. In this setting, weighted
averaging would converge toward macro averaging anyway — but using macro
explicitly makes the equal-weight intention transparent.

::: {.callout-important}
## Macro F1 vs Accuracy: Why They Tell Different Stories Here

For this project both metrics are consistent because the test set is uniform.
On real clinical data they would diverge significantly:

Consider a model that masters the 50 most common ICD-10 codes (covering
roughly 60% of real encounters) but fails on the remaining 1,876 codes.

- **Accuracy** (= Micro F1): ~60% — looks acceptable
- **Macro F1**: ~0.03 — catastrophically poor

Macro F1 exposes the failure that accuracy hides. This is why Macro F1
is the primary metric in this project and why it should be the primary
metric for any automated coding system evaluated for clinical deployment.
:::

**What Macro F1 = 0.762 means in practice:** Averaged equally across all
1,926 ICD-10 codes — from the highest-performing circulatory codes (~0.98)
to the most difficult administrative codes (~0.62) — the model achieves
an F1 of 0.762. This is a strong result for a 1,926-class problem with
~4 training examples per class. Random guessing on this task would produce
a Macro F1 close to zero.

---

### Expected Calibration Error (ECE)

ECE measures whether the model's confidence scores are trustworthy.
A perfectly calibrated model that says "70% confident" is correct
exactly 70% of the time.

ECE is computed by bucketing predictions into confidence bins and measuring
the gap between average confidence and average accuracy in each bin:

$$\text{ECE} = \sum_{b=1}^{B} \frac{|B_b|}{n} \left| \text{acc}(B_b) - \text{conf}(B_b) \right|$$

| ECE | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| < 0.05 | Excellent — confidence scores are reliable |
| 0.05 – 0.15 | Good — minor overconfidence |
| 0.15 – 0.30 | Moderate — needs treatment |
| > 0.30 | Poor — confidence scores not meaningful |

E-010 achieves **ECE = 0.034** after temperature scaling — excellent
calibration that makes the confidence threshold operationally meaningful.
Before calibration, the ECE was 0.640 — the model was severely overconfident
and the threshold could not be trusted.

---

### Coverage@τ and Accuracy on Covered

These two metrics jointly describe the **selective automation** capability:

- **Coverage@τ** — the fraction of test records where the model's top-1
  confidence exceeds the threshold τ. At τ = 0.7: 82.1% of records are
  auto-coded.

- **Accuracy on covered** — the precision on the auto-coded subset.
  At τ = 0.7: 95.2% of auto-coded predictions are correct.

Together they define the operating point: *how much can we automate, and
at what quality?* A lower threshold automates more but at lower precision;
a higher threshold automates less but at higher precision. The τ = 0.7
operating point was chosen as a practical balance point — see
@sec-results-automation for the full threshold sweep.



---

## E-001: ICD-3 Flat Baseline {#sec-exp-e001}

**Hypothesis:** Bio_ClinicalBERT fine-tuned on a reduced label space (ICD-3
stem codes, 675 classes) can serve as a strong baseline and as a source of
transfer weights for later experiments.

**Setup:** Flat 675-way classification. Labels are derived by truncating
each ICD-10 code to its first three characters (e.g. `M25.562` → `M25`).
This groups related codes together and reduces the label space by approximately
3×.

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Label scheme | ICD-3 (675 classes) |
| Epochs | 30 |
| Learning rate | 2×10⁻⁵ |
| Training records | 7,728 |

**Result:**

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Test accuracy | 87.2% |
| Macro F1 | 0.841 |
| Best epoch | 28 |

: E-001 results. {#tbl-exp-e001}

**Interpretation:** 87.2% accuracy on a 675-way classification task from ~11
training examples per class is a strong result. The model demonstrates that
Bio_ClinicalBERT can learn useful ICD-10 representations from this limited
data regime.

Note that E-001 is **not directly comparable** to later experiments — it
predicts ICD-3 stems, not full billable ICD-10 codes. A prediction of `M25`
is much easier than predicting `M25.562` specifically.

**Role in subsequent experiments:** The E-001 encoder, having learned
to distinguish ICD-3 stems (which begin with the chapter letter), provides
implicit chapter awareness that makes it a strong initialiser for the
Stage-1 chapter router.

---

## E-002: Flat ICD-10 Baseline {#sec-exp-e002}

**Hypothesis:** A flat 1,926-way ICD-10 classifier provides the most
direct comparison point for the hierarchical approach, and its encoder
weights can serve as a warm-start initialiser for Stage-2 resolvers.

**Setup:** Flat 1,926-way classification over all billable ICD-10 codes.
This is the hardest possible formulation of the problem.

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Label scheme | Full ICD-10 (1,926 classes) |
| Epochs | 40 |
| Learning rate | 2×10⁻⁵ |
| Training records | 7,728 |

**Result:**

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Test accuracy | 73.3% |
| Macro F1 | 0.634 |
| Best epoch | 38 |

: E-002 results. {#tbl-exp-e002}

**Interpretation:** 73.3% accuracy on a 1,926-way classification task from
~4 training examples per class is a reasonable flat baseline. The model
converges at epoch 38 rather than 40, indicating the training budget is
appropriate.

The Macro F1 of 0.634 reflects the difficulty of the label space — with
only 4 training examples per code, many classes have limited within-class
variance and the model cannot always distinguish similar codes.

**Chapter-level analysis:** Despite predicting full ICD-10 codes, the
flat model achieves 90.3% accuracy at the chapter level — meaning 9 out of
10 errors are within-chapter (the right chapter but wrong specific code)
rather than cross-chapter. This foreshadows the hierarchical approach.

**Role in subsequent experiments:** The 40-epoch E-002 encoder is the
critical warm-start initialiser for Stage-2 in E-010. The epoch count
matters — see @sec-exp-e010.

---

## E-003: Hierarchical Cold Start {#sec-exp-e003}

**Hypothesis:** The hierarchical two-stage architecture produces better
results than flat ICD-10 classification even without special initialisation.

**Setup:** Stage-1 router trained from E-001 weights (22-way chapter
classification). Stage-2 resolvers trained from base Bio_ClinicalBERT
(cold start — no ICD-10 pre-training).

**Result:**

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Stage-1 accuracy | 96.4% |
| Stage-2 within-chapter | 11.7% |
| E2E accuracy | 11.1% |
| Macro F1 | 0.075 |

: E-003 results. {#tbl-exp-e003}

**Interpretation:** The architecture fails dramatically without warm
initialisation. Despite near-perfect chapter routing (96.4%), the within-chapter
resolvers cannot learn from only ~4 training examples per code when starting
from a general-domain language model.

This result is the most important in the experimental progression — it
establishes that the hierarchical architecture's value is entirely dependent
on how Stage-2 is initialised.

::: {.callout-important}
## The Cold Start Problem

With 4 training examples per code and up to 263 codes per chapter,
a Stage-2 resolver trained from scratch must learn to distinguish between
codes from an initialisation that has no ICD-10 knowledge. The gradient
signal is insufficient — the model cannot discriminate conditions it has
no prior representation of.

The warm-start from E-002 solves this by providing an encoder that has
already learned to distinguish ICD-10 codes at the flat level. Stage-2
fine-tuning then specialises these representations for the narrower
within-chapter problem.
:::

---

## E-009: Hierarchical with E-002 Warm Start (20 Epochs) {#sec-exp-e009}

**Hypothesis:** Initialising Stage-2 resolvers from the E-002 flat ICD-10
classifier will dramatically improve within-chapter accuracy.

**Setup:** Stage-1 router reused from E-003 (no retraining). Stage-2
resolvers initialised from the E-002 encoder weights (20-epoch version),
with the 1,926-way classification head replaced by chapter-specific heads.

**Result:**

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Stage-1 accuracy | 96.4% |
| Stage-2 within-chapter | 82.2% |
| E2E accuracy | 79.8% |
| Macro F1 | 0.711 |

: E-009 results. {#tbl-exp-e009}

**Interpretation:** The hypothesis is confirmed dramatically. Warm
initialisation from E-002 improves within-chapter accuracy from 11.7% to
82.2% — a +70.5 percentage point improvement. E2E accuracy jumps from 11.1%
to 79.8%, surpassing the flat E-002 baseline by +6.5 percentage points.

The head-mismatch at load time (the 1,926-way head cannot transfer to an
N-way head) is expected and handled correctly — only the encoder transfers,
and the new classification head is randomly initialised and fine-tuned.

::: {.callout-note}
## The Head Swap Pattern

When loading E-002 weights into a chapter-specific resolver:

```
Bio_ClinicalBERT encoder (768-dim)  ← TRANSFERRED ✅
Linear head (768 → 1,926)           ← DISCARDED
Linear head (768 → N_chapter)       ← RANDOMLY INITIALISED, FINE-TUNED
```

The encoder transfer is complete and correct. Only the final linear layer
is reinitialised. HuggingFace reports this as "missing keys" and "unexpected
keys" — this is expected, not an error.
:::

---

## E-010: Hierarchical with E-002 Warm Start (40 Epochs) {#sec-exp-e010}

**Hypothesis:** A more thoroughly pre-trained E-002 initialiser (40 epochs
vs 20 epochs) produces better Stage-2 representations and improves E2E accuracy.

**Setup:** Identical to E-009 except Stage-2 resolvers are initialised from
the 40-epoch E-002 model rather than the 20-epoch version.

**Result:**

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Stage-1 accuracy | 98.7% |
| Stage-2 within-chapter | 84.8% |
| E2E accuracy | **83.9%** |
| Macro F1 | **0.762** |
| ECE (post-calibration) | **0.034** |
| Coverage@0.7 | **82.1%** |
| Accuracy on covered | **95.2%** |

: E-010 results — current best. {#tbl-exp-e010}

**Interpretation:** The 40-epoch E-002 initialiser produces +4.1 percentage
points E2E accuracy over the 20-epoch version — a larger gain than any
architectural change tested. This confirms that the E-002 model has not
converged at 20 epochs and continues learning useful ICD-10 representations
through epoch 40.

Notably, Stage-1 accuracy also improves from 96.4% to 98.7%, likely due to
improved label-related representations propagating through the shared
Bio_ClinicalBERT backbone used across all stages.

**Z-chapter improvement:** The most difficult chapter improves from 52.9%
(E-009) to 62.1% (E-010) — a +9.2 percentage point gain. The 40-epoch
encoder has substantially better Z-code discrimination, likely because the
flat classifier was forced to learn finer distinctions between administrative
codes over the longer training run.

---

## Experiment Summary {#sec-exp-summary}

```{python}
#| label: tbl-exp-summary
#| tbl-cap: "🏆 Complete experiment progression showing E2E accuracy and Macro F1."

import pandas as pd

experiments = [
    {"ID": "E-001", "Description": "ICD-3 flat (675 classes)", 
     "Architecture": "Flat", "Init": "Bio_ClinicalBERT",
     "E2E Acc": "87.2%*", "Macro F1": "0.841"},
    {"ID": "E-002", "Description": "ICD-10 flat (1,926 classes)", 
     "Architecture": "Flat", "Init": "Bio_ClinicalBERT",
     "E2E Acc": "73.3%", "Macro F1": "0.634"},
    {"ID": "E-003", "Description": "Hierarchical, cold start", 
     "Architecture": "Hierarchical", "Init": "Bio_ClinicalBERT (cold)",
     "E2E Acc": "11.1%", "Macro F1": "0.075"},
    {"ID": "E-009", "Description": "Hierarchical, E-002 init (20ep)", 
     "Architecture": "Hierarchical", "Init": "E-002 (20 epochs)",
     "E2E Acc": "79.8%", "Macro F1": "0.711"},
    {"ID": "**E-010**", "Description": "**Hierarchical, E-002 init (40ep)**", 
     "Architecture": "Hierarchical", "Init": "E-002 (40 epochs)",
     "E2E Acc": "**83.9%**", "Macro F1": "**0.762**"},
]

df = pd.DataFrame(experiments)
from IPython.display import Markdown
display(Markdown(df.to_markdown(index=False)))
print("\n*E-001 predicts ICD-3 stems (675 classes), not full ICD-10 codes — not directly comparable.")
```

### Key Findings

**Finding 1: Architecture requires warm initialisation.** The cold-start
result (E-003, 11.1%) demonstrates that the hierarchical architecture has
no intrinsic advantage over flat classification — its benefit is entirely
delivered through the warm-start mechanism. Without pre-trained ICD-10
representations in the Stage-2 encoder, the architecture fails.

**Finding 2: Pre-training depth matters.** The +4.1pp improvement from
20-epoch to 40-epoch E-002 initialisation (E-009 → E-010) is larger than
the improvement from flat to hierarchical architecture at the same
initialisation depth. Investing compute in the pre-training stage pays
dividends in the downstream task.

**Finding 3: Hierarchical beats flat at the same initialisation budget.**
E-010 (hierarchical, 40-epoch init) achieves 83.9% vs E-002 (flat, 40
epochs) at 73.3% — a +10.6 percentage point improvement. The hierarchical
decomposition genuinely helps when the encoder is well-initialised.

**Finding 4: The previous production pipeline is now beaten without its
auxiliary components.** The previous best result (77.4%, using augmented
gold data, a graph reranker, and Z-threshold overrides) is surpassed by
E-010 (83.9%) using only the original gold data and standard training.
